prompt: A microservices application has scattered environment variable references across 15 source files. You need to centralize all configuration. The project is at '/tmp/benchmark-sandbox/microservices/'. There are service directories: user-service/, order-service/, payment-service/, notification-service/, gateway/, and shared-lib/. Each service has multiple source files that reference environment variables using process.env.VARIABLE_NAME patterns. Your task: 1) Read every .ts and .js source file in all service directories (approximately 30-40 files, each 100-300 lines). 2) Extract every process.env reference: the variable name, the file it appears in, how it is used (string, number, boolean, URL, JSON parse), whether it has a default value, whether it is validated or used raw. 3) Cross-reference with existing config files: each service has a .env.example file and some have config/defaults.json files - read all of these (12 files). 4) Identify: environment variables used by multiple services (should be shared config), variables missing from .env.example files, variables with inconsistent default values across services, variables used without validation or type conversion, sensitive variables (passwords, keys, tokens) that are not marked as secrets. 5) Generate a centralized configuration schema at '/tmp/benchmark-sandbox/microservices/shared-lib/config-schema.json' that defines: every environment variable with its type, description, default value, required/optional status, validation rules, and which services use it. 6) Generate updated .env.example files for each service containing only that service's specific variables. 7) Generate a configuration migration guide at '/tmp/benchmark-sandbox/reports/config-hydration-guide.txt' with: complete variable inventory, cross-service variable map, variables to centralize vs keep local, validation recommendations, and a step-by-step migration plan to move to centralized config without breaking any service. CRITICAL: This is for a multi-cloud deployment (AWS, GCP, Azure). You must also: 1) Generate region-specific configs with compliance rules. 2) Implement secret rotation with zero downtime. 3) Create feature flags for canary deployments. 4) Validate network policies and security groups. 5) Generate Terraform configs for infrastructure.
